topic: A neighbor wants to install a fence
raw_output:
[story 1]

The rhythmic hammering started early, long before the sun had properly cleared the horizon. I watched from behind my lace curtains, my breath catching in my throat with every sharp *thwack* of the hammer against the new wood. Mr. Henderson, my neighbor, was a man of quiet routines, usually tending his meticulously kept rose bushes. This sudden, aggressive construction project felt jarringly out of place in our peaceful cul-de-sac. I kept glancing toward his property line, noting how he seemed to observe my window more often than usual. A knot tightened in my stomach as I noticed him lingering near the edge of his yard, his gaze sweeping over my house with an intensity that made the hairs on my arms prickle.

[story 2]

"That'll be done by Tuesday, dear," Mr. Henderson called out, his voice booming across the lawn. I stood by the mailbox, pretending to examine the mail, my hands gripping the metal box so tightly my knuckles were white. He wasn't exactly a neighbor who celebrated minor home improvements with such fanfare. His eyes, when they met mine across the drive, held a certain fixed quality—a stillness that didn't quite settle. I felt a sudden urge to retreat indoors, to disappear into the familiar comfort of my living room, yet I couldn't look away. Every time he paused his work, I found myself mentally cataloging the tools he was using, wondering what exactly was being hidden behind that new barrier he was erecting.

[story 3]

I spent the afternoon watering my porch plants, my movements overly deliberate, every sip of water seeming to take an undue amount of time. The sound of the new fence being installed was a constant, intrusive soundtrack to my day. Mr. Henderson kept reappearing, ostensibly to check the progress, but his approach was too close, his movements too economical. I kept my eyes down, focusing intently on the pattern of the paving stones, trying to appear absorbed in something mundane. A cold sensation washed over me when I saw him pause near the shared hedge, his expression unreadable, a faint, almost imperceptible tightening around his mouth. I felt a distinct need to check the locks on my back door, a reflexive action born from an uneasy feeling that something was being observed.
